title: How to Map BC Mineral Claims from the Public Registry
metaDescription: Search British Columbia mineral claims by company or claimholder, import the tenures into Exploration Maps, style them, and export a clean investor-ready map. Includes Mineral Titles Online verification steps.
directAnswer: To map BC mineral claims, open Exploration Maps, search a company or claimholder to pull their British Columbia tenures from public registry data, add the claims to the map, assign the Claims layer role for standard styling, add context and a title block, then export a PNG or PDF. Always confirm current ownership, status, and boundaries in BC Mineral Titles Online (MTO) before relying on the map.

ctaTop.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
ctaTop.sub: Pull a company's BC tenures and have a styled map in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, drop in your claims, and export a clean PNG or PDF.

sections.body: You can build a BC claims map two ways: search a claimholder directly inside Exploration Maps, or import a tenure file you already have. Either way it helps to have:
checklist: A company or claimholder name (for the in-app claim search), or
A BC tenure file you exported from MTO or a GIS (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Your company logo (PNG or JPG) for the title block
The project name and map date
The official MTO tenure numbers you want to verify

sections.h2: Step 1: Search BC claims by company or claimholder
sections.body: Open Exploration Maps and choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry. Select British Columbia, then type a company or claimholder name. The tool returns the matching tenures from public BC registry data so you can map a competitor's ground or your own holdings without manually entering coordinates.

image.alt: Claim search panel with British Columbia selected and a company name typed in
image.caption: Searching BC tenures by claimholder name in the Add Claims panel

sections.h2: Step 2: Add the claims to your map
sections.body: Select the tenures (or tenure groups) you want and click Add to map. They're added as a claims layer and the map fits to their extent automatically. If you instead have a tenure file, use the import button in the Layers section and select your GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML — the layer is added the same way.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Add a claimholder's BC tenures to the map in one click.

sections.h2: Step 3: Assign the Claims role and style the tenures
sections.body: Expand the layer card and choose 'Claims' from the Role dropdown to apply the standard convention: a medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill. Adjust the stroke and fill colour pickers if you want to distinguish optioned ground, JV claims, or separate blocks — each layer gets its own legend entry, so rename them to something descriptive like 'Optioned Claims' or 'Zone 1 Tenures'.

image.alt: Layer card showing the Claims role selected with blue stroke and fill
image.caption: Assigning the Claims role applies investor-standard blue styling

sections.h2: Step 4: Add context, a title block, and your logo
sections.body: Enable the Context overlay (roads, towns, water) and Reference Labels in the Design section so viewers can orient the property, and drop the overlay opacity to 40–60% so the claims stay dominant. Click the title on the map to enter the project and company name, set the map date in Design → Text & Metadata, and upload your logo at the bottom of the Layers section.

sections.h2: Step 5: Export a clean BC claims map
sections.body: Pick an export ratio — Landscape 16:9 for slides and news releases, Letter Portrait for reports — frame the claims within the bounds, then export PNG for presentations or PDF for print. Enter your email to unlock watermark-free exports.

image.alt: Export panel with Landscape 16:9 ratio selected over a framed BC claims map
image.caption: Framing and exporting the finished BC claims map

sections.h2: Example: mapping a competitor's Golden Triangle ground
sections.body: Say you want a one-slide map of a peer's claims near the Golden Triangle for a corporate update. Search their company name, select British Columbia, add their tenure group, assign the Claims role, switch to the Satellite basemap to show the terrain, add a title block with your logo and the date, then export Landscape 16:9. Total time is a few minutes versus an afternoon of downloading shapefiles and styling them in a desktop GIS.

sections.h2: Verify everything in Mineral Titles Online
sections.body: Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool, not the official registry. Before you publish or rely on a BC claims map, confirm the details directly in MTO.

box.title: Verify in BC Mineral Titles Online (MTO)
box.html: Always verify current ownership, status, expiry, good-to dates, and boundaries for each tenure number in BC Mineral Titles Online before relying on the map. Tenure status can change daily.

sections.h2: Where the claim data comes from

box.title: Data source & disclaimer
box.html: Pulls from public registry data where available. Some jurisdictions are queried live; others use refreshed public datasets. BC tenure information originates with the Province of British Columbia's Mineral Titles Online. Exploration Maps is not affiliated with or endorsed by the Government of British Columbia and is not the official registry of record.

sections.h2: What the app can and can't verify

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: show tenure geometry and attributes from public data, let you style and label them, and combine them with your own files.
Cannot: confirm legal ownership, good-standing, work-requirement compliance, or that boundaries are survey-accurate. Treat the map as a visualization, and confirm specifics in MTO.

sections.html: Next, see the broader how to make a mining claims map guide, compare workflows in Ontario, learn to import a GeoJSON or Shapefile, and when you're done, export a print-ready PDF. You can also start straight from the BC mineral claims map tool page.

faqs.q: Can I search BC mineral claims by company name?
faqs.a: Yes. In Exploration Maps, choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, select British Columbia, and type a company or claimholder name. Matching tenures from public registry data are returned so you can add them to your map without entering coordinates. Always confirm the results against BC Mineral Titles Online.

faqs.q: Is Exploration Maps the official BC claims registry?
faqs.a: No. Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool. BC's official registry of record is Mineral Titles Online (MTO), operated by the Province of British Columbia. Use Exploration Maps to build and export maps, and verify ownership, status, and boundaries in MTO.

faqs.q: How current is the BC claim data?
faqs.a: It reflects public registry data, which may be queried live or drawn from refreshed public datasets depending on the jurisdiction and source. Tenure status can change daily, so always verify current status and good-to dates in MTO before relying on the map.

faqs.q: What file formats can I import for BC tenures?
faqs.a: GeoJSON, Shapefile (.shp, zipped), and KML/KMZ. If you have corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, export it as CSV and use the Column Mapper to assign latitude and longitude. You can convert Shapefiles to GeoJSON for free at mapshaper.org.

faqs.q: Can I show my claims and a competitor's claims on the same map?
faqs.a: Yes. Add each set as its own layer, give them different stroke and fill colours, and rename them so they read clearly in the legend (for example 'Our Claims' and 'Adjacent Tenures'). Each layer appears as a separate legend entry.

faqs.q: What's the standard colour for mineral claims on a BC map?
faqs.a: A medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill — the convention used in most NI 43-101 technical reports. It's applied automatically when you assign the Claims role, and you can override it per layer.

title: How to Make a Mining Claims Map
metaDescription: The complete guide to making a mining claims map: import public claim data, shapefiles, KML, GeoJSON, or drillhole CSVs, style your claims, and export a clean investor-ready map in minutes. Links to province-by-province search guides.
directAnswer: To make a mining claims map, get your claims into Exploration Maps — either search a claimholder to pull public registry data or import a GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML — assign the Claims layer role for standard blue styling, add context layers and a title block, then export as PNG or PDF. Exploration Maps is a visualization tool, not the official registry, so verify ownership and boundaries with the relevant provincial registry.

ctaTop.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
ctaTop.sub: Search a claimholder or import a file and style it in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor and have a shareable claims map in minutes.

sections.h2: What you need before you start
sections.body: A mining claims map needs claim geometry plus a little context. Gather:
checklist: Claim data: a claimholder name to search, or a boundary file (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Optional context: roads, water, or infrastructure files
Your company logo (PNG or JPG)
Project name, map date, and any required disclaimers

sections.h2: Find your province's claim-search guide first
sections.body: The fastest way to map claims is to search a claimholder and pull public registry data straight into the map. Each province's registry works a little differently — start with the guide for your jurisdiction, then come back here to style and export.

sections.h2: Step 1: Get your claims onto the map
sections.body: Open Exploration Maps. To search public data, choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, pick your province, and type a company or claimholder name. To use your own file, click the import button in the Layers section and select your GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML. Either way the layer is added automatically and the map fits to your claims. If your data is corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, import the CSV and the Column Mapper assigns the fields.

image.alt: Layers panel with a claims boundary imported and the map fit to its extent
image.caption: A claims boundary imported and framed automatically

sections.h2: Step 2: Assign the Claims layer role
sections.body: Expand the layer card and select 'Claims' from the Role dropdown. This applies the standard convention automatically: a blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill — the styling investors and NI 43-101 reviewers expect. Override the stroke and fill colours per layer if you're showing multiple blocks.

image.alt: Layer card with the Claims role selected showing blue claim styling
image.caption: The Claims role applies investor-standard styling in one click

sections.body: A claims map without context is hard to read. Enable the Context overlay (roads, towns, water) and Reference Labels in the Design section, and reduce overlay opacity to 40–60% so your claims stay dominant. Add roads or infrastructure files if you have them.

sections.h2: Step 4: Choose a basemap and theme
sections.body: For investor presentations and reports, the Light basemap with the Investor — Navy & White theme is the standard choice. Use the Satellite basemap to show terrain, and the Technical — Sharp Borders theme for formal regulatory filings.

sections.h2: Step 5: Configure the title block and logo
sections.body: Click the title on the map to edit it inline — project name as the title, company name as the subtitle. Add the map date and project number in Design → Text & Metadata, and upload your logo at the bottom of the Layers section.

cta.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
cta.sub: Pull public tenures for any province and style them instantly.

sections.body: Activate an export ratio — Landscape 16:9 for slides and news releases, Letter Portrait for technical reports — frame your claims within the bounds, then export PNG for presentations or PDF for print. Enter your email to unlock watermark-free exports.

Suppose you hold a core claim block and recently optioned a second block. Import (or search) both, put each on its own layer, colour the core block solid blue and the optioned block in a contrasting outline, rename the layers 'Core Claims' and 'Optioned Ground', add roads for access context, drop in your logo and date, and export Landscape 16:9. You now have a clean property overview suitable for a deck or a news release.

box.title: Data source & disclaimer
box.html: Pulls from public registry data where available. Some jurisdictions are queried live; others use refreshed public datasets. Exploration Maps is a visualization and workflow tool, not the official registry of record for any province.

sections.h2: Verify before you rely on the map

box.title: Always verify with the official registry
box.html: Always verify current ownership, status, expiry, and boundaries with the official provincial registry before relying on the map — for example BC Mineral Titles Online, Ontario MLAS, Quebec GESTIM, or Newfoundland GeoAtlas.

sections.html: Build investor visuals with the investor presentation guide, add drill data with the drill results map guide, learn imports in the GeoJSON / Shapefile guide, and finish with the export to PDF guide.

faqs.q: What file format do I need for mining claims data?
faqs.a: GeoJSON is preferred, and Shapefile (zipped) and KML/KMZ also work. You can convert Shapefiles to GeoJSON for free at mapshaper.org or in QGIS. If you only have corner coordinates in a spreadsheet, export it as CSV and use the Column Mapper to assign latitude and longitude. Or skip files entirely and search a claimholder to pull public registry data.

faqs.q: Do I need to know the claim coordinates, or can I search by company?
faqs.a: You can search by company or claimholder for supported provinces — choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, pick the province, and type the name. The matching public tenures are added to your map without entering coordinates. Always verify the results in the official provincial registry.

faqs.q: What is the standard colour for mineral claims on a map?
faqs.a: A medium-blue stroke with a light, semi-transparent blue fill (around 20–25% opacity). It's applied automatically when you assign the Claims role and matches the convention used in most NI 43-101 technical reports.

faqs.q: Can I export a mining claims map as a PDF?
faqs.a: Yes. Choose the PDF export and select your page size (Letter Landscape, A4 Landscape, or News Release Figure). PDFs are vector-quality and suitable for print in regulatory filings and technical reports. See the export to PDF guide for details.

faqs.q: How do I show multiple claim blocks with different colours?
faqs.a: Import or search each block as its own layer, then set a different stroke and fill colour per layer. Each layer becomes a separate legend entry, so rename them to something descriptive like 'Zone 1 Claims' or 'Optioned Claims'.

faqs.q: Is Exploration Maps the official claims registry?
faqs.a: No. It's a visualization and workflow tool that helps you turn public claim data and your own files into clean maps. The official registries of record are the provincial systems (BC MTO, Ontario MLAS, Quebec GESTIM, Newfoundland GeoAtlas, and others). Always verify ownership, status, and boundaries there before relying on the map.

title: How to Make a Drill Results Map
metaDescription: Turn a drill collar CSV into a clean drill results map for news releases and NI 43-101 reports: required columns, fixing coordinate issues, hole-ID callouts and intercept labels, and a print-ready export.
directAnswer: To make a drill results map, import your drill collar CSV, map the latitude/longitude (or easting/northing) columns, assign the Drillholes role, add callout labels showing hole ID and best intercept, add the property boundary, then export as a Landscape 16:9 PNG or a news-release figure PDF.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Drop in a collar CSV and label your best holes in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn drill data into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, import your collars, and export a news-release figure.

sections.body: A drill results map is built from a collar table plus your headline intercepts. Gather:
checklist: A drill collar CSV (one row per hole) with hole ID and coordinates
Your best intercept per key hole (width × grade) for the labels
A property/claims boundary file for context (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Your company logo and the program/map date

sections.body: At minimum you need a hole ID and a coordinate pair. Everything else is preserved as feature properties you can show in callouts.

sections.h2: Step 1: Import the drill collar CSV
sections.body: Click the import button in the Layers section and select your collar CSV. The Column Mapper opens automatically — assign latitude and longitude. Columns named Lat, Long, Latitude, Longitude, Easting, or Northing are detected for you. Every other column (hole ID, depth, grade) is preserved and accessible when you click a hole.

image.alt: Column Mapper showing latitude and longitude columns being assigned from a collar CSV
image.caption: Mapping coordinate columns when importing a collar CSV

sections.body: Most drill maps that come out wrong are a coordinate problem, not a styling problem.
checklist: Lat/long swapped: if holes land in the wrong hemisphere, you've mapped longitude to latitude — re-map the columns
UTM vs decimal degrees: easting/northing in the hundreds of thousands won't plot — convert to decimal degrees (WGS84) first, then import
Sign dropped: western/southern coordinates must be negative (e.g. -122.5, not 122.5)
Datum mismatch: NAD83 vs WGS84 is usually sub-metre, but a wrong datum or zone can shift holes by hundreds of metres — verify against a known landmark

sections.body: Expand the layer card and select 'Drillholes' from the Role dropdown for standard dark circle markers. A marker size of 12–16 px reads well at most scales.

image.alt: Drillholes role selected on a layer of collar points over a property
image.caption: Collars styled with the Drillholes role

sections.h2: Step 3: Add hole-ID and intercept callouts
sections.body: Click a hole to open the inline feature editor. Enter the hole ID as the title and the best intercept as the subtext — for example '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au' — then Add / Update Callout. For your top results, switch the callout type to 'Badge Label' and put the grade in the badge chip so the standout holes pop. Use the Priority field to keep labels from colliding on dense programs.
Import your claims boundary as a second layer and assign the Claims role. This gives the drill pattern the property context investors and analysts expect to see.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Add your claims boundary around the drill pattern in seconds.

sections.h2: Step 5: Frame and export for a news release
sections.body: Activate Landscape 16:9 — the standard for news-release figures — and frame so all holes and key callouts fit. The Investor — Navy & White theme with a Light or Satellite basemap is the convention. Export PNG at 2× for email and web, or the News Release Figure PDF (6" × 4.5") for print-ready delivery.

You've finished a 12-hole program with three standout intercepts. Import the collar CSV, map the coordinates, assign Drillholes, badge-label the three best holes with their grades, add plain callouts on the rest, drop in the claims boundary and your logo, switch to Satellite to show the ridge the holes follow, and export Landscape 16:9. That figure is ready to attach to the news release and reuse in the deck.

sections.h2: About the data and what to disclose

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: plot the collars you provide, preserve your attributes, and label intercepts exactly as you enter them.
Cannot: validate assays, QA/QC, or that an intercept is true width. The map reflects the data you import — make sure your disclosure follows your QP's guidance and applicable securities rules.

sections.html: See the news-release figure workflow, the broad mining claims map guide, how to import CSV data, and how to export a print-ready PDF.

faqs.q: What columns does my drill collar CSV need?
faqs.a: At minimum a hole ID and a latitude/longitude pair in decimal degrees (WGS84). If your collars are in UTM, convert them to decimal degrees first. Total depth and best intercept are recommended because they feed your callouts, and any extra columns are kept as feature properties you can display.

faqs.q: My holes plotted in the wrong place — what happened?
faqs.a: Almost always a coordinate issue: latitude and longitude swapped, a missing negative sign on western/southern coordinates, or UTM easting/northing imported as if they were decimal degrees. Convert UTM to decimal degrees (WGS84) first, re-open the Column Mapper, confirm the columns, and re-import.

faqs.q: What format should drill results be shown in on a map?
faqs.a: The standard is '[width]m @ [grade] [unit] [element]' — for example '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au' or '82m @ 0.92% Cu'. Put the hole ID as the callout title and the intercept as the subtext.

faqs.q: How many drill holes should I label?
faqs.a: Label all current-program holes plus key historical holes for context. If it gets crowded past ~20 holes, use the Priority field to control which callouts render on top and reserve Badge Labels for your top 3–5 intercepts.

faqs.q: Should I use a satellite or light basemap?
faqs.a: Both work. Satellite conveys terrain and setting and is popular for news releases; Light is cleaner for formal technical reports and PDF attachments in NI 43-101 documents.

faqs.q: Can I show historical and current holes differently?
faqs.a: Yes — import them as separate layers and style them differently (filled dark markers for current, hollow or lighter for historical). Name the layers so they appear as separate legend entries.

title: How to Create an Exploration Map for an Investor Presentation
metaDescription: Build investor-ready exploration maps for decks, project overviews, and news releases: pick a branded theme, set a 16:9 layout, keep the legend clean, add callouts, and export sharp PNGs and PDFs.
directAnswer: For an investor presentation map, use a branded theme (such as Investor — Navy & White), the Landscape 16:9 ratio, a satellite or light basemap, a concise legend, and callouts for your key results — then export PNG at 2× resolution. Most decks use three maps: a regional location map, a property/claims overview, and a results map.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Build a deck-ready map with your brand colours and logo in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor and export sharp PNGs and PDFs for your deck.

sections.h2: Why map quality matters in mining investor decks
sections.body: Professional investors and analysts evaluate dozens of exploration companies. A clean, consistent, on-brand map signals operational competence; a blurry or cluttered one undermines confidence in the technical team. Your map should communicate the property's merits at a glance — even on a projector from the back of the room.

sections.h2: The three maps most decks need
sections.body: Rather than reusing one map everywhere, build a small set that each does one job.
checklist: Regional location map — where the project sits relative to known camps and infrastructure
Property / claims overview — your tenure footprint with a clean legend
Results or targets map — drill intercepts or target areas with callouts

sections.h2: Step 1: Brand the map with a theme, colours, and logo
sections.body: Pick a theme such as Investor — Navy & White, then apply your own brand colours and logo so every map in the deck matches. Saving a brand kit means the next project starts on-brand automatically. Click the title to set the project and company name, and add the date in Design → Text & Metadata.

image.alt: Map with a navy title block, company logo, and branded colours applied
image.caption: A branded title block keeps every deck map consistent

sections.body: Activate Landscape 16:9 in the Export section before framing — it matches the standard PowerPoint and Keynote slide, so the map fills the slide with no letterboxing. The frame shows exactly what will export, so pan and zoom to taste.

image.alt: Export panel with Landscape 16:9 selected and the map framed to the slide
image.caption: Framing to the 16:9 slide ratio before export

sections.body: Decks are read at speed. Disable legend entries the audience doesn't need, use the Compact legend mode, and if you have more than ~5 entries, split into two slides (overview + detail) rather than crowding one map.

cta.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
cta.sub: Pull your tenures and build a clean property-overview slide.

sections.h2: Step 4: Add callouts for your headline results
sections.body: Use Badge Label callouts for top drill intercepts so they pop, and plain labels for target names. Keep text short — '32m @ 6.1 g/t Au' beats a full sentence. These are the numbers the room remembers.

sections.h2: Step 5: Export sharp assets for the deck
sections.body: Export PNG at 2× pixel ratio (a ~3840×2160 image — sharp on screen and in print handouts) and insert it as an image so it travels with the deck. For investor one-pagers and printed overviews, export a PDF instead. Reuse the same maps in news releases for a consistent look.

Heading into a financing, you need three slides. Make a regional location map on Satellite showing proximity to a producing mine, a claims overview on Light with a compact legend, and a results map badge-labelling your three best holes. Brand all three with one kit, export each at 16:9 2×, and you have a consistent map set for the deck, the one-pager, and the website — built in well under an hour.

sections.h2: A note on commercial use and exports

box.title: Exports & commercial use
box.html: Exploration Maps is built for commercial exploration work — investor decks, project overviews, and news-release figures. Watermark-free exports unlock with your email, and paid export/subscription options may be offered. See the export guide for formats and resolutions.

box.title: Verify before you present
box.html: If your map shows claims or tenures, always verify current ownership, status, and boundaries with the official registry before presenting to investors. Exploration Maps is a visualization tool, not the official registry.

sections.html: Build the underlying maps with the mining claims map guide and the drill results map guide, prep a release figure with the news-release guide, and export with the PDF export guide.

faqs.q: What size should a mining map be in a PowerPoint slide?
faqs.a: Export at Landscape 16:9 with 2× pixel ratio. That yields roughly a 3840×2160 image — sharp at any zoom. Insert it as a full-slide image with no white border.

faqs.q: How many maps should I include in an investor presentation?
faqs.a: Usually 2–3: a regional location map, a property/claims overview, and a detailed drill-results or target map. Each does a different job — avoid reusing one map for all three.

faqs.q: How do I keep all my deck maps looking consistent?
faqs.a: Apply the same theme, brand colours, and logo to each map. Saving a brand kit lets new projects start on-brand automatically, so a regional map, a claims map, and a results map all match.

faqs.q: Should the company logo be on the map itself?
faqs.a: Yes for anything that leaves a controlled deck — news releases, website downloads, email attachments. On a branded slide template the deck logo may be enough, but a logo on the map protects the asset when it's extracted and reused.

faqs.q: Can I use these maps in a news release as well as a deck?
faqs.a: Yes — that's a common workflow. Export the same map at 16:9 PNG for the deck and as a news-release figure PDF for the release, so the investor materials stay visually consistent. See the news-release guide for the figure workflow.

title: How to Import GeoJSON Data into a Mining Map
metaDescription: Import GeoJSON into a mining map in one click. Convert Shapefiles free at mapshaper.org, assign layer roles for automatic styling, and fix common coordinate-system issues.
directAnswer: To import GeoJSON into Exploration Maps, click the import button in the Layers section, select your .geojson or .json file, and the layer is added automatically with the map zoomed to your data. Assign a layer Role (Claims, Drillholes, Targets) to get standard exploration styling in one click.

checklist: A .geojson or .json file (polygons, lines, or points)
Coordinates in WGS84 latitude/longitude (EPSG:4326) — the standard for web maps
If your data is a Shapefile or KML: two minutes to convert it (below)

sections.h2: Why GeoJSON is the easiest format to work with
sections.body: GeoJSON is a single text file that encodes polygons, lines, and points as JSON. Unlike a Shapefile — which is really four or more files that must travel together — a GeoJSON never arrives missing its .prj or .dbf. Most government open-data portals and modern GIS tools export it directly, and it imports into Exploration Maps with zero configuration.

sections.h2: Converting a Shapefile to GeoJSON (free, 2 minutes)
sections.body: If your data came as a Shapefile (.shp + .dbf + .shx + .prj) or KML, convert it first:
items: Go to mapshaper.org (free, runs in your browser — your data never uploads to a server)
Drag the whole Shapefile set in (or the .zip) — include the .prj so the projection is read correctly
Click Export → GeoJSON
Import the downloaded file into Exploration Maps

sections.body: In Exploration Maps, click the import button in the Layers section of the sidebar and select your file. The layer is added, the map zooms to its bounds, and polygon, line, and point geometry are detected automatically. Attribute properties come along too — they drive labels and callouts later.

sections.h2: Assign a Role to get instant styling
sections.body: Expand the new layer card and set its Role. Roles apply the styling conventions readers of exploration maps expect — Claims get the classic boundary treatment, Drillholes render as collar points, Target Areas get dashed outlines. You can override any colour afterwards, but the role gets you to a presentable map immediately.

image.alt: Assigning the Claims role to an imported GeoJSON layer
image.caption: Assigning a Role after import applies standard exploration styling in one click.

sections.h2: If your data lands in the wrong place
sections.body: Exploration Maps expects geographic coordinates (WGS84 / EPSG:4326). If your imported layer appears in the ocean or at (0,0), the file is almost certainly in a projected system like UTM.

box.title: Fix a UTM or projected file
box.html: Open the original file in mapshaper.org, run the console command -proj wgs84 (or export from your GIS with CRS set to EPSG:4326), then re-import. Zone mismatches are the #1 cause of misplaced exploration data.

cta.text: Import your GeoJSON and export an investor-ready map.
cta.sub: Drag the file in, pick a role, export in minutes.

faqs.q: What GeoJSON geometry types can I import?
faqs.a: Polygons and MultiPolygons (claims, licences, target outlines), LineStrings (roads, powerlines, trenches), and Points (drill collars, samples, showings). A single FeatureCollection can mix types, though separate layers per type style more cleanly.

faqs.q: Is there a file size limit for GeoJSON imports?
faqs.a: Very large files (tens of megabytes) can slow the browser. If your file is heavy, simplify the geometry at mapshaper.org (Simplify → 10–30%) — claim boundaries rarely need survey-grade vertex density for a presentation map.

faqs.q: Do my GeoJSON attributes/properties import too?
faqs.a: Yes. Feature properties are preserved and available for labels and callouts — for example a claim number or hole ID column can be shown directly on the map.

faqs.q: My coordinates are in UTM. Can I import them?
faqs.a: Convert to WGS84 first. In mapshaper, load the file with its .prj and export as GeoJSON with the wgs84 setting, or reproject in QGIS with 'Save As' → CRS EPSG:4326. If the file has no .prj, you'll need to know the UTM zone to reproject correctly.

faqs.q: Can I import multiple GeoJSON files into one map?
faqs.a: Yes — each import becomes its own layer with independent styling, visibility, and legend entry. A typical project map stacks a claims layer, a drillhole layer, and a target-areas layer.

title: How to Choose the Right Basemap for a Mining Map
metaDescription: Which basemap for a mining map? Light for technical reports, Satellite for investor decks, Topographic for access maps, Dark for social — with a quick-reference table.
directAnswer: Use the Light basemap for NI 43-101 reports and regulatory filings, Satellite for investor presentations that benefit from terrain context, Topographic for access and infrastructure maps, and Dark for digital dashboards and social media. When in doubt: Light for print, Satellite for slides.

rows: NI 43-101 figures, permitting, any printed document
You need terrain or vegetation context
Investor decks, location maps, showing access and topography
The document will be printed in black and white
Access roads, infrastructure planning, field maps
Print of any kind — dark backgrounds waste ink and lose detail

sections.h2: Light — technical reports and regulatory filings
sections.body: A neutral light-grey canvas that makes data layers the star: claim boundaries, drill collars, and labels all read clearly, and it photocopies and prints without losing information. If the map is going into a technical report, a permit application, or anything a regulator will read, start here.

sections.h2: Satellite — investor presentations and location maps
sections.body: Real imagery gives an audience instant context — terrain, vegetation, lakes, existing roads and disturbance. It answers the questions investors actually ask ('how remote is this?', 'is there access?') before they're asked. Pair it with white or light-coloured boundaries and labels so they hold contrast against the imagery.

Contour lines and elevation shading make this the right choice when the story is physical: road access, proposed drill pads, powerline routes, camp locations. Field crews also find topo maps the easiest to orient with.
High contrast makes coloured layers glow on screen — striking for a website hero, a social post announcing results, or an on-screen dashboard. Never print it.
Whichever basemap you pick, two overlays sharpen the story:
items: Context overlay — roads, water bodies, and towns at low opacity
Reference Labels — place names that orient a reader instantly

box.title: One deck, one basemap
box.html: If a presentation includes several maps, keep the basemap consistent across all of them — switching styles slide-to-slide reads as sloppy. Pick per document, not per map.

faqs.q: Which basemap is best for an NI 43-101 technical report?
faqs.a: Light. It prints cleanly, keeps data layers legible, and matches the neutral presentation regulators and QPs expect in report figures. Use Satellite only for a supplementary location/access figure where imagery adds real information.

faqs.q: Can I change the basemap after styling my layers?
faqs.a: Yes — basemap and data layers are independent, so you can switch any time. Do check label and boundary contrast after switching: white labels that pop on Satellite can disappear on Light.

faqs.q: Which basemap should I use for a drill results news release figure?
faqs.a: Satellite or Light. Satellite gives context for a property-scale figure; Light is safer for a tight collar-map figure where grades and hole IDs must be unmissable.

faqs.q: Do the basemaps work at any zoom level?
faqs.a: Yes, all four cover the globe at property to continental scales. Satellite resolution varies by region at very tight zooms — remote northern properties may show coarser imagery than populated areas.

faqs.q: Is there a blank/no-basemap option for clean claim plats?
faqs.a: Use Light at low opacity or crop tightly — its neutral grey reads nearly blank in print while keeping just enough geographic anchoring.

title: How to Export a Mining Map as a PDF
metaDescription: Export a mining map as a print-ready PDF: page sizes for NI 43-101 figures and news releases, 3× pixel ratio for print, and watermark-free download.
directAnswer: To export a mining map as a PDF, set your ratio in the Export section (Letter, A4, or 16:9), click the PDF button in the toolbar, choose the matching page size, enter your work email to remove the watermark, and download. Use 3× pixel ratio for anything that will be printed.

checklist: Frame the view — everything important inside the ratio guides, nothing critical near the edges
Title block filled in: project, company, date
Legend, scale bar, and north arrow enabled
Ratio set to match your target page size (Portrait 3:4 for Letter Portrait, Landscape 16:9 for slides)

sections.body: Use PDF for anything printed or filed — technical report figures, permitting documents, news-release figures that go to a wire service. Use PNG for slides, websites, and email. PDFs preserve exact page dimensions; PNGs are lighter and drop straight into PowerPoint.
Activate the matching ratio in the Export section first — the on-screen guides show exactly what the PDF will contain, so you frame once and export without surprises.

image.caption: Match the on-screen ratio to your PDF page size before exporting.

box.title: 3× for print, 2× for screens
box.html: In Export Settings, set Pixel Ratio to 3× for any printed PDF — roughly 300 DPI equivalent, so fine claim boundaries and small labels stay crisp on paper. 2× is fine for PDFs that will only be viewed on screen.

sections.body: Enter your work email in the export dialog to unlock clean, watermark-free exports. You only do this once per browser.

faqs.q: What resolution is the exported PDF?
faqs.a: The map is embedded at your chosen pixel ratio: 2× is sharp on screens; 3× is print quality (~300 DPI equivalent at the chosen page size). For NI 43-101 report figures, use 3×.

faqs.q: Can I export a PDF sized for a news release?
faqs.a: Yes — the News Release Figure size (8 × 6 in) matches what wire services and IR teams typically request, and pairs with the Landscape ratio guides for framing.

faqs.q: Is the PDF vector or raster?
faqs.a: The map itself is a high-resolution raster embedded at exact page dimensions — consistent output regardless of layer complexity, and what report printers expect. Use 3× pixel ratio to keep it print-crisp.

faqs.q: Why does my exported PDF crop differently from my screen?
faqs.a: The export follows the ratio guides, not your browser window. Activate the ratio that matches your page size before framing, and keep key content inside the guide lines.

faqs.q: Can I export multiple page sizes from the same map?
faqs.a: Yes — the map isn't altered by exporting. Switch ratio/page size and export again; a common workflow is a 16:9 PNG for the deck plus a Letter PDF for the report from the same project.

title: How to Add a Distance Ring to a Mining Map
metaDescription: Add a distance ring to a mining map: place the centre, set a true-distance radius in km, label the arc, and use outside-shade to focus attention on your project.
directAnswer: Click 'Draw Distance Ring' in the Annotations section, click the centre point on the map, set the radius in kilometres, and add a curved arc label such as '50 km from Port of Prince Rupert'. Rings are drawn as true great-circle distances.

sections.h2: When a distance ring earns its place
sections.body: Distance rings turn 'remote project' into a concrete number. Use one when proximity is part of the story:
items: Distance to the nearest town, port, rail line, mill, or power line
'Within trucking distance' arguments for development-stage projects
Showing several projects within a set radius of shared infrastructure
District-scale maps — '25 km from the discovery' positioning

sections.body: Click 'Draw Distance Ring' in the Annotations section, then click the map where the ring should be centred — the infrastructure item is usually the better centre than your project, because the label then reads as the distance to it. Set the radius in kilometres in the ring controls; the ring is computed as a true great-circle distance, so it stays accurate at any latitude.
Enable 'Curved arc label' and type the text — e.g. '50 km from Port of Prince Rupert'. The label follows the arc, so it stays readable without covering your data. Keep it short: distance + landmark.

box.title: Outside shade
box.html: Enable 'Outside shade' to dim everything beyond the ring with a translucent vignette. It pulls the eye inside the radius — effective on satellite basemaps where imagery competes with your data. Keep the shade subtle; the goal is focus, not blackout.

faqs.q: Is the ring a true distance circle or a screen circle?
faqs.a: A true great-circle radius — 50 km on the ring is 50 km on the ground, regardless of latitude or how the web-mercator basemap stretches at high latitudes.

faqs.q: Can I add more than one distance ring?
faqs.a: Yes. Concentric rings (e.g. 25/50/100 km) from one centre work well for regional context; separate rings around different infrastructure points each carry their own label.

faqs.q: Should the ring centre on my project or the infrastructure?
faqs.a: Centre on the infrastructure when the message is 'we are X km from it' — the ring then passes through or near your project, which reads naturally. Centre on the project when showing everything available within a radius.

faqs.q: Does the ring appear in the exported PNG/PDF?
faqs.a: Yes — rings, arc labels, and outside shade all render in exports exactly as on screen, at full export resolution.

title: How to Add a Scale Bar and North Arrow to a Mining Map
metaDescription: Add a scale bar and north arrow to a mining map in two clicks, position them by corner, and meet the standard expectations for NI 43-101 report figures.
directAnswer: Toggle Scale Bar and North Arrow on in the Design section of the sidebar. Both render on the map automatically — the scale bar computes true ground distance for your current view — and each can be assigned to any corner with the corner pickers.

sections.h2: Why these two elements are non-negotiable
sections.body: A map without a scale bar and north arrow isn't a figure — it's an illustration. Reviewers of technical reports expect both on every map figure, and investors subconsciously read their presence as competence. They cost two clicks; missing them costs credibility.

box.title: Report figures
box.html: Preparing figures for a technical report? See the full checklist in How to Create an NI 43-101 Map Figure.

sections.body: Open the Design section and toggle Scale Bar on. It calculates real ground distance for the current view and picks a clean round number (1 km, 2 km, 5 km…) that updates as you zoom — you never hand-edit it.
Toggle North Arrow on in the same section. Choose the style that matches the map's tone — the simple arrow suits technical figures; the compass style suits presentation maps.

sections.h2: Position both with the corner pickers
sections.body: Each element gets a corner assignment. The conventions that keep a figure tidy:
items: Scale bar: bottom-left or bottom-right, away from the legend
North arrow: a top corner, opposite the title block
Never let either overlap data, labels, or the inset map
Elements assigned to the same corner stack automatically with correct spacing

faqs.q: Does the scale bar stay accurate at high latitudes?
faqs.a: Yes — it computes true ground distance at the map centre for the current view rather than assuming a fixed metres-per-pixel, which is exactly where naive scale bars go wrong on northern properties.

faqs.q: Can I set the scale bar to a specific distance like exactly 5 km?
faqs.a: The bar auto-selects a clean round distance appropriate to your zoom. Adjust zoom slightly and it snaps between standard steps (1, 2, 5, 10 km…), so you can land on the figure you want.

faqs.q: Are the scale bar and north arrow included in exports?
faqs.a: Yes, both render into PNG and PDF exports at full resolution, in the corners you assigned.

faqs.q: Do I need both for an NI 43-101 figure?
faqs.a: Standard practice is yes — scale bar, north arrow, legend, and title information are the baseline expectations for technical report figures. Your QP has final say on the report's requirements.

title: How to Create a Target Generation Map
metaDescription: Create a target generation map: dashed target outlines over claims, labelled rationale per target, disclosure-safe wording, and an investor-ready export.
directAnswer: To make a target generation map, import your claims and target outlines, assign the Target Areas role for dashed-outline styling, add drill collars or geochem points if you have them, label each target with a name and one-line rationale, then export. Frame targets as conceptual — they are exploration targets, not resources.

checklist: Claim/property boundary (GeoJSON, Shapefile, or KML)
Target outlines — drawn in your GIS, or sketched from geophysics/geochem
Optional: drill collars, geochem points, or geophysical anomaly outlines that support each target
A one-line rationale per target (e.g. 'coincident IP chargeability + Au-in-soil')

items: Import the property boundary and assign the Claims role
Import target outlines and assign the Target Areas role — they get the conventional dashed outline that reads as 'conceptual, not drilled'
Add supporting layers (collars, soil grids) so targets visibly sit on evidence
Label each target: short name plus the rationale — 'Target A — IP anomaly, 1.2 g/t Au boulders'
Choose Satellite or Topographic basemap so targets sit in real terrain context
Add title block, legend, scale bar, north arrow; export 16:9 PNG for decks or PDF for reports

rows: Dashed = interpreted/conceptual; solid fill overstates confidence
The one 'hard' legal line on the map
Evidence, not the story — keep them quiet
Soft colour fill at low opacity
Background support for the targets drawn over them

box.title: Targets are conceptual — label them that way
box.html: An exploration target is not a mineral resource. Keep wording like 'exploration target', 'untested anomaly', or 'conceptual' on the map or its caption, and avoid grades/tonnage on target labels unless they carry the required cautionary language. Your QP should review any figure that goes into public disclosure.

cta.text: Turn your target concepts into a clean map.
cta.sub: Import boundaries, drop in targets, export an investor-ready figure.

faqs.q: How do I draw target outlines if I only have points or a grid?
faqs.a: Sketch the outlines in your GIS (QGIS's polygon tool over your geophysics/geochem raster works well), export as GeoJSON, and import. Many teams simply digitize a loose ellipse around the anomaly — the dashed styling correctly communicates that it's interpretive.

faqs.q: What's the difference between a target generation map and a drill plan?
faqs.a: A target map shows where and why you would drill (anomalies, rationale); a drill plan shows exactly where holes are placed. Target maps are conceptual and dashed; drill plans show engineered collar positions.

faqs.q: Can I show geophysics under my targets?
faqs.a: Yes — import anomaly outlines as polygons at low-opacity fill, then draw targets above. Evidence under interpretation is the layout that convinces.

faqs.q: How many targets should one map show?
faqs.a: As many as the property genuinely has — but rank them (A/B/C or 1/2/3) in the labels. A map with 15 unranked targets reads as unfocused; the same 15 with a clear top-3 reads as a pipeline.

faqs.q: Is a target generation map suitable for a news release?
faqs.a: Yes, commonly — but the disclosure caution applies double. Keep target labels free of implied tonnage/grade, mark outlines as conceptual, and have your QP review the figure before release.

title: How to Create an Infrastructure Map for a Mining Property
metaDescription: Create a mining infrastructure map: roads, power, rail and facilities layered over your property, distance rings to ports and towns, and a clean export for studies and decks.
directAnswer: To make an infrastructure map, import your property boundary, add roads, powerlines, rail, and facilities as line and point layers, style them in muted colours under a Topographic or Satellite basemap, add distance rings to key infrastructure, and export. The story is access and cost — make distances explicit.

checklist: Property/claims boundary
Infrastructure line data — roads, powerlines, rail (government open-data portals publish these for most jurisdictions)
Point locations for facilities: towns, ports, mills, airstrips, camps
The distances that matter to your story (to power, to port, to labour)

items: Import the property boundary (Claims role)
Import each infrastructure type as its own layer — separate layers mean separate legend entries and independent styling
Style lines by type: solid for existing roads, dashed for proposed routes; distinct colours for power vs rail
Add facility points with labels (port, mill, town names)
Choose the Topographic basemap — terrain context explains why routes go where they go
Add distance rings from key infrastructure (e.g. '35 km from grid power')
Title block, legend, scale bar, north arrow — then export

box.title: Numbers beat lines
box.html: Investors don't measure your map — they read it. A road on the map says access exists; a distance ring labelled '12 km to Highway 37' says what it costs. Put explicit distances on every piece of infrastructure that matters.

cta.sub: Import boundaries and infrastructure, label the distances, export.

faqs.q: Where do I get roads and powerline data?
faqs.a: Provincial and state open-data portals publish road networks and, in most cases, transmission lines as downloadable GIS layers (Shapefile or GeoJSON). National datasets cover rail. Convert to GeoJSON at mapshaper.org if needed and import each as its own layer.

faqs.q: How do I show proposed vs existing infrastructure?
faqs.a: The universal convention: existing = solid lines, proposed = dashed. Add '(proposed)' to the legend label too — a study reviewer will look for it.

faqs.q: What basemap works best for infrastructure maps?
faqs.a: Topographic. Contours explain route choices (valleys, passes) and give field-realistic context. Satellite is the alternative when vegetation/terrain imagery itself is part of the access story.

faqs.q: Should an infrastructure map show my drill targets too?
faqs.a: Usually not — one map, one story. Keep the infrastructure figure clean and pair it with a separate targets or results map in the same deck, using the same basemap and theme for consistency.

faqs.q: Can I use this for a PEA or feasibility-style figure?
faqs.a: Yes — a clean property-plus-infrastructure figure with explicit distances is a standard early-study exhibit. Export as Letter PDF at 3× pixel ratio for the document, and have the study author confirm figure requirements.

title: How to Import CSV Data into a Mining Map
metaDescription: Import drill collars, soil geochem, and rock samples from a CSV into Exploration Maps: required columns, fixing UTM and lat/long coordinate issues, labelling points, and exporting a clean map.
directAnswer: To import CSV data into a mining map, click import and select your CSV — the Column Mapper opens automatically. Assign your latitude and longitude columns (in decimal degrees), click Import, and your points render immediately. Every other column is preserved as a feature property you can use for labels and callouts.

ctaTop.text: Import your file and export an investor-ready map.
ctaTop.sub: Drop in a CSV, map the coordinates, and style it in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn your spreadsheet into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, import your CSV, and export a PNG or PDF.

sections.h2: What CSV data works with Exploration Maps
sections.body: Any CSV with a coordinate pair works. The most common exploration uses are:
checklist: Drill collars: Hole ID, Latitude, Longitude, Total Depth, Best Intercept
Soil geochemistry: Sample ID, Latitude, Longitude, Au (ppb), As (ppm)
Rock samples: Sample ID, Latitude, Longitude, Rock Type, Grade
Prospects / showings: Name, Latitude, Longitude, Type, Notes
Historical workings: Name, Latitude, Longitude, Type (shaft, adit, open cut)

sections.body: Only the coordinate pair is strictly required. Column names can be anything — you assign them in the Column Mapper.

rows: Decimal degrees; negative in the west
Grade, depth, element — kept as properties
Useful for styling or splitting layers

sections.body: Use decimal degrees (e.g. 49.2827, -123.1207). The first row must be headers, with no blank rows above it, and any text fields containing commas should be wrapped in double quotes. If your data is in UTM (easting/northing), convert it to decimal degrees first — see the coordinate-issues section below.

sections.h2: Step 2: Import and open the Column Mapper
sections.body: Click the import button in the Layers section and select your CSV. The Column Mapper opens automatically, listing every detected column with dropdowns to assign roles.

image.alt: Column Mapper listing CSV columns with latitude and longitude dropdowns
image.caption: The Column Mapper opens automatically for any imported CSV

sections.body: Use the Latitude and Longitude dropdowns. Columns named Lat, Latitude, Y, or POINT_Y (and Long, Longitude, X, POINT_X) are auto-detected; assign anything else manually. Click Import and your points render on the map immediately.

cta.text: Turn your spreadsheet into a clean map.
cta.sub: Map your coordinate columns and see points appear instantly.

sections.h2: Step 4: Set the role and style
sections.body: Expand the layer and assign a role: Drillholes for collars, Anomalies for geochem, or Default for general points. Override marker colour, size, and shape in the styling controls. For soil results, sizing markers by grade communicates spatial distribution at a glance.

image.alt: Imported point layer with a role assigned and marker size set
image.caption: Assigning a role and styling imported points

sections.h2: Step 5: Label points and add callouts
sections.body: Click any point to open the inline feature editor — all your CSV columns are available as properties. Put the hole or sample ID in the label, and for drill holes put the best intercept in the subtext (e.g. '15.2m @ 4.8 g/t Au'). For headline results, use a Badge Label so the number stands out.
Most failed imports are coordinate problems, not file problems.
checklist: UTM instead of decimal degrees: easting/northing in the hundreds of thousands won't plot — convert to decimal degrees (WGS84) first in QGIS or an online converter
Lat/long swapped: points land in the wrong hemisphere — re-map the columns
Sign dropped: western longitudes (and southern latitudes) must be negative — -123.1, not 123.1
Degrees-minutes-seconds: convert to decimal degrees (DD = D + M/60 + S/3600)
Commas inside a field: wrap text values containing commas in double quotes so columns don't shift
Blank rows above the header: delete them so the first row is the header

sections.body: You have a 400-sample soil grid as a CSV with Sample ID, Lat, Long, and Au (ppb). Import it, map the coordinates, assign the Anomalies role, size the markers by the Au column so anomalies read instantly, label your top samples, add the claims boundary for context, and export Landscape 16:9. A spreadsheet becomes a presentation-ready anomaly map in a few minutes.

sections.h2: What the app does with your data

box.title: Can and cannot verify
box.html:
Can: plot points from your coordinates and keep every column as a property for labels.
Cannot: reproject from a local grid or UTM for you (convert to decimal degrees first), or validate the underlying assays/QA-QC. The map reflects exactly the data you import.

sections.html: Use this with the drill results map guide and the mining claims map guide, or import vector files with the GeoJSON / Shapefile guide. Finish with the export to PDF guide.

faqs.q: What column names are recognized automatically?
faqs.a: For latitude: Lat, Latitude, lat, latitude, Y, POINT_Y. For longitude: Long, Longitude, lon, long, longitude, X, POINT_X. Anything else you assign manually in the Column Mapper dropdowns.

faqs.q: My data is in UTM, not latitude/longitude. Can I import it?
faqs.a: Not directly — Exploration Maps expects decimal degrees (WGS84). Convert first: open the CSV in QGIS, set the layer CRS to your UTM zone (e.g. EPSG:32610 for UTM Zone 10N), and export as GeoJSON in EPSG:4326. For small datasets, an online UTM-to-decimal converter works.

faqs.q: Can one CSV hold both drill collars and soil samples?
faqs.a: Import one layer per CSV. If a single file mixes types, split it into separate CSVs and import each as its own layer so you can style and role them independently.

faqs.q: What happens to my extra columns (Grade, Depth, Hole ID)?
faqs.a: They're preserved as feature properties on each point. They aren't shown on the map by default, but appear when you click a point — copy any value into the label or subtext to display it.

faqs.q: Can I import a CSV from Leapfrog, GEMS, or a mine-planning system?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as the export includes decimal-degree coordinate columns. If your system uses a local grid or UTM, reproject to WGS84 decimal degrees (e.g. via QGIS) before importing.

faqs.q: Does importing a CSV change the original file?
faqs.a: No. The import reads your CSV into the map; your source spreadsheet is untouched. The map stores its own copy of the points and properties.

title: How to Create an NI 43-101 Compliant Map
metaDescription: Build NI 43-101-ready map figures: the standard element checklist (scale, north arrow, legend, datum, date), where each lives in Exploration Maps, and a print-quality export.
directAnswer: An NI 43-101 report map figure is expected to include a title with project and figure number, scale bar, north arrow, legend, coordinate/projection note, data source, effective date, and clean legible styling. Exploration Maps includes all of these elements — turn them on, fill in the title block, and export as PDF at 3× pixel ratio.

sections.body: Technical report figures follow strong conventions. Before any map goes to your QP, it should carry:
checklist: Title block: project name, figure title, figure number
Company name and map date (effective date of the data)
Scale bar (true ground distance) and north arrow
Legend covering every symbol and line style on the figure
Location/index inset so the reader can place the map
Data sources (e.g. 'Claim boundaries: BC Mineral Titles Online, retrieved 2026-07-06')
Legible styling in print: no reliance on subtle colour differences

sections.h2: Where each element lives in Exploration Maps

rows: Shows project, subtitle, and date; add figure number to the title text
Every visible layer can carry a legend entry — audit before export
Province/state inset places the property automatically
E.g. 'WGS84 / Web Mercator. Claims: MTO, 2026-07-06. Not an official tenure record.'

items: Import claims/drillholes/targets and assign roles so styling follows convention
Choose the Light basemap — it prints cleanly and keeps data prominent
Fill the title block: project, figure title and number, company, date
Enable scale bar, north arrow, legend, and the location inset
Add the footer source/datum note
Set Letter or A4 ratio, frame the figure, export PDF at 3× pixel ratio

box.title: Your QP decides — this is figure craft, not legal advice
box.html: NI 43-101 is a disclosure standard, and the Qualified Person signing the report decides what its figures must show. The checklist above reflects standard expectations reviewers look for, not a legal guarantee of compliance. Always have the QP review final figures — and verify tenure data against the official registry before it appears in a report.

cta.text: Build a report-ready figure in minutes.
cta.sub: Standard elements on by default — title block, scale, legend, inset.

faqs.q: Does NI 43-101 formally list required map elements?
faqs.a: The instrument requires that maps be legible and support the disclosure (location, tenure, results), and Form 43-101F1 requires certain maps (e.g. location, property). The specific elements — scale, north arrow, legend, sources — are standard professional practice that QPs and reviewers expect on every figure.

faqs.q: What projection/datum note should I use?
faqs.a: State what the map uses — for Exploration Maps exports that's WGS84 geographic data rendered in Web Mercator. A footer like 'WGS84 / Web Mercator; claim boundaries from [registry], retrieved [date]' covers datum, source, and currency in one line.

faqs.q: Can I use a satellite basemap in a technical report figure?
faqs.a: Yes, where imagery adds information (access, terrain, disturbance) — typically the location/access figure. For claim plats and results figures, the Light basemap keeps line work legible in print.

faqs.q: What export settings make a figure print-ready?
faqs.a: PDF at Letter or A4 page size with pixel ratio 3× — roughly 300 DPI equivalent. Check that the smallest text on the figure is still readable at 100% print size.

faqs.q: Is registry claim data reliable enough for a report figure?
faqs.a: Registry pulls are the right starting point, but tenure changes; verify ownership, status, and boundaries against the official registry as of the report's effective date, and date-stamp the source in the figure. The report's QP has final responsibility for the data shown.

faqs.q: Do I need a location inset on every figure?
faqs.a: Not every figure — but the property-location figure needs one, and small-scale detail figures benefit from one so readers stay oriented. It's one toggle, so the practical answer is: include it unless it crowds the figure.

title: How to Search Ontario Mining Claims and Map Them
metaDescription: Search Ontario mining claims by company name or claim number from public MLAS data, add them to a map, style them, and export a clean investor-ready figure. Includes MLAS verification steps.
directAnswer: To search Ontario mining claims, open Exploration Maps, choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, select Ontario, type a company or claim-holder name (or a claim number), pick your claim groups, and click Add to map. Assign the Claims role, style, and export. Always confirm current holder, status, and boundaries in the official Ontario MLAS before relying on the map.

ctaTop.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
ctaTop.sub: Pull an Ontario company's claims and style them in minutes.

ctaBottom.text: Turn public claim data into a clean map.
ctaBottom.sub: Open the editor, add Ontario claims, and export a PNG or PDF.

sections.body: The Ontario Mineral Lands Administration System (MLAS) is the provincial registry for mining claims, leases, and other mineral dispositions, administered by the Ontario Ministry of Mines. It covers the whole province, with active exploration concentrated in the Abitibi Greenstone Belt, Timmins–Kirkland Lake, and the Sudbury area. Exploration Maps uses public MLAS claim data so you can map tenure without downloading shapefiles.

sections.h2: Step 1: Open the claims registry search for Ontario
sections.body: From the landing page choose Add Claims → Search Claims Registry, or open '+ Add Claims' in the editor sidebar. Select Ontario — MLAS from the province dropdown.

image.alt: Add Claims panel with Ontario — MLAS selected in the province dropdown
image.caption: Selecting Ontario in the claims registry search

sections.h2: Step 2: Search by company name or claim number
sections.body: Type a company or individual holder name — e.g. 'Alamos Gold' or 'Agnico Eagle' — in the Company tab and press Enter, or switch to the Claim # tab for a specific Ontario claim number. Results are grouped by geographic cluster (Kirkland Lake, Timmins, Thunder Bay) so you can pick the right tenure package quickly.

cta.text: Search a claimholder and create a map.
cta.sub: Find an operator's Ontario ground and add it in one click.

sections.h2: Step 3: Add the claims and assign the Claims role
sections.body: Check the groups you want — the summary shows total claim count and area in hectares — then click Add to map. The claims load as a polygon layer, and assigning the Claims role applies the standard blue styling used in NI 43-101 figures. Rename layers if you're showing more than one operator.

sections.h2: Step 4: Style, add context, and export

title: What to Include on a Mining Claim Map
metaDescription: The complete checklist for a professional mining claim map — boundaries, labels, title block, legend, scale, context, inset, and the verification note most maps forget.
directAnswer: A mining claim map needs: styled claim boundaries, claim labels, a title block with project/company/date, a legend, scale bar and north arrow, geographic context (roads, water, place names), a location inset, and a registry-verification note. That combination makes it readable, credible, and safe to share.

checklist: Claim boundaries — the subject, styled conventionally
Claim labels — tenure numbers or block names
Title block — project, company, date
Legend — every symbol explained
Scale bar + north arrow
Geographic context — roads, water, towns at low opacity
Location inset — where in the province/state this sits
Registry note — where the data came from and when

sections.body: Claims are the legal spine of the map — style them so nothing competes: the conventional treatment is a solid boundary with a light fill. If the property has multiple claim blocks or partners, colour by block/owner and let the legend explain.
Label claims with tenure numbers (technical audiences) or block names (investor audiences). Unlabelled polygons force readers to cross-reference a table — label the map and the figure stands alone.

image.alt: A styled mining claims map with labels and legend
image.caption: Boundaries, labels, and a legend — the core of a claim map that stands alone.

sections.h2: Title block and date: the credibility markers
sections.body: Project name, company, and — critically — a date. Tenure changes monthly; an undated claim map is unusable to anyone careful. The date tells readers exactly how current the picture is.

These five turn a shape-drawing into a map: scale bar and north arrow orient it; the legend decodes it; low-opacity roads/water/towns place it locally; the inset places it regionally. All are toggles in the Design section — there's no excuse for a figure missing them.

box.title: Say where the boundaries came from
box.html: Add a footer note: source registry and retrieval date, plus 'verify current tenure with the official registry'. E.g. 'Claim boundaries: BC Mineral Titles Online, retrieved 2026-07-06. Not an official tenure record.' It protects you, and sophisticated readers look for it.

faqs.q: Should I show expired or pending claims on the map?
faqs.a: Only with distinct styling and a legend entry — e.g. dashed grey for 'application pending'. Mixing expired ground into a solid claims layer misrepresents the holding; when in doubt, map active tenure only.

faqs.q: What colour should claim boundaries be?
faqs.a: There's no rule, but the readable convention is a strong boundary colour (blue/red) with a light fill of the same hue at low opacity. On satellite basemaps, use white or a bright colour for contrast.

faqs.q: Do I label every claim or just blocks?
faqs.a: Scale decides. A 10-claim property: label each tenure number. A 300-cell land package: label named blocks and provide the full tenure list in an accompanying table — 300 numbers on one figure is noise.

faqs.q: What goes in the legend of a claims-only map?
faqs.a: Even a single-layer map benefits from a legend entry ('Mineral claims — Company X') plus entries for context layers if shown. If claims are coloured by owner or block, the legend is where that coding is explained.

faqs.q: Is a claim map from public registry data accurate enough to publish?
faqs.a: For presentations and releases, yes, with the source-and-date note and registry verification of anything material. For legal or transaction purposes, always work from official registry records — public GIS layers can lag.

rows: Claims/target boundaries from GIS and registries
It's 4+ files — a missing .prj loses the projection
Large files can be slow; simplify heavy geometry
Google Earth sketches, some registry exports
Styling is discarded on import; geometry survives
Drill collars, samples — any point list
Coordinate column naming and UTM vs lat/long

sections.body: Decades old and still what most registries and consultants hand over. A 'shapefile' is really a bundle: .shp (geometry), .dbf (attributes), .shx (index), .prj (projection). Keep the set together — especially the .prj, without which no tool can know where your data belongs. Convert to GeoJSON at mapshaper.org (drag the whole set or a .zip in, export GeoJSON) for the smoothest import.

One text file, human-readable, no companion files to lose, imports directly. When a consultant or data room asks what format you want, say GeoJSON in WGS84. Its only weakness is bulk — survey-grade boundaries carry more vertices than a presentation map needs, and mapshaper's Simplify fixes that in seconds.

KML (and its zipped sibling KMZ) is how boundaries get sketched and shared by people who don't run GIS. Geometry imports fine; Google Earth's styling (icons, colours) doesn't — you'll restyle with layer roles anyway, which is faster than fixing inherited styles.

A spreadsheet with latitude/longitude columns is the natural format for drill collars, soil samples, and showings. On import you map which columns are coordinates and identifiers — hole ID and coordinates are enough; extra columns (depth, azimuth, assay) survive for labels and callouts.
sections.html: For the collar-specific workflow — column mapping, callouts, badge labels — see How to Import CSV Data into a Mining Map.

box.title: When in doubt: mapshaper.org
box.html: Free, browser-based, nothing uploads to a server. It opens Shapefile/GeoJSON/KML/CSV, reprojects (-proj wgs84), simplifies heavy geometry, and exports clean GeoJSON. It untangles 90% of exploration data problems.

faqs.q: Which file format should I request from a consultant or data room?
faqs.a: GeoJSON in WGS84 (EPSG:4326). It's one file, opens everywhere, and imports with zero configuration. Shapefile is an acceptable second — insist the .prj is included.

faqs.q: My Shapefile imports in the wrong location. What happened?
faqs.a: It's in a projected coordinate system (usually UTM) and either the .prj is missing or wasn't honoured. Load it into mapshaper with its .prj and export as WGS84 GeoJSON; if there's no .prj you'll need to know the UTM zone to reproject correctly.

faqs.q: Can I import a KMZ directly?
faqs.a: KMZ is a zipped KML. If a KMZ doesn't import, unzip it (rename to .zip) and import the .kml inside — the geometry is identical.

faqs.q: What columns does a CSV need for drill collars?
faqs.a: Minimum: a hole ID and a coordinate pair (latitude/longitude in decimal degrees, or easting/northing you convert first). Recommended: depth, azimuth, dip, and your headline intercept for labels.

faqs.q: Does converting between formats lose data?
faqs.a: Geometry and attributes survive Shapefile ↔ GeoJSON ↔ KML conversion. What you lose: Shapefile column-name length limits (10 chars) truncate long attribute names, and KML styling never transfers — plan to restyle after import regardless.
